what_it_does: Acceptance test for RHM operating modes (the presence dial). It discovers the rhm_mode node type, verifies there are eight modes, checks that switching modes edits a node parameter in the system graph and persists across Suite sessions, rejects unknown modes, confirms every active mode carries a behavioral directive, and proves that the 'off' mode disables the RHM by returning a canned reply without calling the model and still logs the turn.
observations: Uses a temporary FsStore and discovers nodes from the repo nodes directory.
Defines a global PASS counter incremented by a local check() helper.
Asserts 'rhm_mode' is present in the registry and suite.MODES has exactly eight entries.
Switches mode via suite.set_mode() and reads it back via suite.get_mode().
Loads the system graph and finds the mode node by suite.MODE_NODE, checking its type and config.mode.
Creates a second Suite over the same store to verify persistence across sessions.
Attempts suite.set_mode('nonsense') and expects a ValueError.
Checks every active mode has a non-empty directive via suite._mode_directive().
Sets mode to 'off' and calls suite.chat(), asserting the reply contains 'off', action is None, mode is reported, and the turn is logged.
standards: Treats the operator's presence preference as a node configuration in the system graph.
The 'off' mode is special: it short-circuits the model entirely and works offline.
Mode switching uses the same create_node/set_config verbs as other node edits.
conventions: Uses sys.path insertion to import from the parent package.
Uses tempfile.mkdtemp with shutil.rmtree cleanup in a try/finally block.
Implements a minimal local assertion helper that also counts passing checks.
Uses global mutable state (PASS) for test tally.
concerns: Relies on Suite internal attributes: MODES, MODE_NODE, _mode_directive(), SYSTEM_GRAPH.
String-based assertions on the 'off' reply and logged text may be brittle if wording changes.
The unknown-mode test raises AssertionError manually if no exception occurs, which is slightly awkward.
Global PASS counter is mutated from inside check(), making parallel execution unsafe.
notes: This file is an end-to-end acceptance test that the RHM mode dial behaves as a persistent, validated node configuration and that 'off' really disables the model.
questions: What are the names of the eight modes in suite.MODES?
What does RHM stand for in this codebase?
How is the mode directive actually injected into the chat behavior?
What is the schema and persistence contract for the system graph?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it verifies modes are nodes, switching edits a parameter, and off disables the RHM.
apparent_themes: Behavioral directives tied to operator presence
intention_signals: Encode the operator's presence preference as a configurable system node switched through the existing node-editing verbs, and prove that mode selection has concrete behavioral consequences including completely disabling the model.
novelty: It treats a UI/persona mode not as a flag but as a node in the system graph, persisted and switched with the same primitives used for other graph nodes.
contribution: Provides an acceptance-level contract for mode lifecycle: discovery, valid mode set, persistence, rejection of invalid modes, directive presence, and the 'off' short-circuit behavior.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test for RHM modes verifying mode-as-node persistence, mode switching via node config, and the 'off' mode disabling model calls while returning a canned reply.
intention_for_embedding: To prove that the RHM presence dial is a persistent node configuration whose modes change real runtime behavior, especially that 'off' works offline.

symbols.does: Local assertion helper that increments a global pass counter and prints an 'ok' line for each passing check.
symbols.description: A minimal test assertion function used throughout the acceptance script. It takes a label and a condition, asserts the condition with a prefixed failure message, increments the global PASS counter on success, and prints a short confirmation line.
